pattern: A running count of distinct subsequences doubles when a new character is appended, but to avoid recounting we subtract the count that existed just before the previous occurrence of that same character.
hints: Build the answer character by character, tracking how many distinct non-empty subsequences exist so far.
Appending a character to every existing subsequence (and to the empty one) doubles the count, but identical characters seen earlier would create duplicates.
Keep dp = total distinct so far; on each character c, new_dp = 2*dp + 1 minus the dp value recorded the last time c appeared, then remember the old dp for c. Apply the modulus throughout.

edge_inputs.description: all distinct characters, every subset counts
a repeated leading character forces duplicate suppression
all identical characters give only length-many subsequences
alternating pattern mixes new and duplicate contributions

stress.description: long random lowercase strings exercising the modular recurrence
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    n = max(1, min(size, 2000))
    return [''.join(rng.choice(string.ascii_lowercase) for _ in range(n))]
stress.note: value range of 26 letters drives frequent duplicate subtraction

stress.description: small alphabet to maximise repeated-character subtraction
